coredata.dc:title: Tracing the entry process of submicrometre plastics in soybean sprouts by leaf-derived fluorescent carbon dots
coredata.dc:creator: Liu, Chao
Jiao, Yuan
Guo, Junmei
Li, Bo
Gu, Changxin
Qian, Tianwei
Liu, Xiaona
coredata.dc:description: As a global emerging contaminant, microplastics (MPs) in water or soil can accumulate in vegetables, making them easily ingested through the diet. With excellent and tunable optical properties, carbon dots (CDs) are highly advantageous for tracing the entry process of MPs. Originally, long-wavelength CDs were synthesized from leaf-derived extracts, and fluorescent submicrometer plastics (CDs-MPs) with clean surfaces and concentrated particle sizes were obtained by soap-free microemulsion polymerization. The concentration of CDs-MPs exhibits a significant linear relationship with long-wavelength fluorescence intensity (λEx/λEm: 415/676 nm). Soybean sprouts (SBS), as an important type of food, are susceptible to contamination of MPs due to their soft epidermis and rapidly growing biomass. The results showed that CDs-MPs could be embedded into the cortex of SBS and enter the plant with cell division and elongation, leading to an increase in pore size on the cell wall surface. After entering the root system, CDs-MPs will pass through the Casparian strip and migrate in the vessels. Then, CDs-MPs enter the leaves through vascular bundles, and the distribution and size of epicuticular wax on leaves have changed. Furthermore, SBS showed resistant growth and increased levels of oxidative response when exposed to MPs/CDs-MPs. It is the first study to demonstrate the application of leaf-derived CDs in the prevention of MPs pollution by revealing the migration behavior of submicrometre plastics in SBS.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Microplastics
Long-wavelength emission
Carbon dots
Fluorescent tracing
Soybean sprouts

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: The exposure of CDs-MPs in soybean sprouts
Observation and measurement of bean sprouts
The characterization and fluorescence properties of leaf-derived CDs
Linear fitting between emulsion concentration and fluorescence intensity
The uptake behavior of CDs-MPs by soybean sprouts
Physiological and biochemical effects of CDs-MPs on soybean sprouts

This work was financially supported by the Applied Basic Research Project of Shanxi Province (No. 20210302124056), Science and technology innovation project of colleges and universities in Shanxi Province (2021L029), and the Fundamental Research Program of Shanxi Province (No. 202303021221041).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: The characterization and fluorescence properties of CDs.
The morphology and characterization of CDs-MPs. (a–c) TEM; (d–f) SEM; (g–l) EDS; (m–o) TG-DSC; (p) FTIR.
The relationship between the concentration and fluorescence intensity of CDs-MPs. (a–b) fluorescence spectrum; (c) polynomial fitting; (d) linear fitting.

Distribution of CDs-MPs in roots, stems, and leaves under CLSM (transverse sections). The excitation wavelength was 415 nm, and the detection wavelength was 676 nm. (a–f) without CDs-MPs; (g–l) CDs-MPs exposed. E, epidermis; Pa, parenchyma cell; Vb, vascular bundle; Lv, leaf veins.
Distribution of CDs-MPs in roots, stems, and leaves was observed by FE-SEM (transverse sections). (a–i) blank control; (j–r) CDs-MPs exposed.

CLSM and SEM characterization of root tissue. (a–f) Blank control under CLSM, longitudinal sections; (g–l) Distribution of CDs-MPs under CLSM, longitudinal sections; (m–o) Distribution of CDs-MPs under FE-SEM, transverse sections. The excitation wavelength was 415 nm, and the detection wavelength was 676 nm.
The growth (a–d) and biochemical indices (e–h) of soybean sprouts under different treatments.

head.ce:title: Tracing the entry process of submicrometre plastics in soybean sprouts by leaf-derived fluorescent carbon dots

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Environmental Science and Engineering, Taiyuan University of Technology, Shanxi Key Laboratory of Earth Surface Processes and Resource Ecological Security in Fenhe River Basin, Shanxi Engineering Research Center of Low Carbon Remediation for Water and Soil Pollution in Yellow River Basin, Jinzhong 030600, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: College of Environmental Science and Engineering, Taiyuan University of Technology, Shanxi Key Laboratory of Earth Surface Processes and Resource Ecological Security in Fenhe River Basin, Shanxi Engineering Research Center of Low Carbon Remediation for Water and Soil Pollution in Yellow River Basin

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: College of Environmental Science and Engineering, Taiyuan University of Technology, Shanxi Key Laboratory of Earth Surface Processes and Resource Ecological Security in Fenhe River Basin, Shanxi Engineering Research Center of Low Carbon Remediation for Water and Soil Pollution in Yellow River Basin, Jinzhong, 030600, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: As a global emerging contaminant, microplastics (MPs) in water or soil can accumulate in vegetables, making them easily ingested through the diet. With excellent and tunable optical properties, carbon dots (CDs) are highly advantageous for tracing the entry process of MPs. Originally, long-wavelength CDs were synthesized from leaf-derived extracts, and fluorescent submicrometer plastics (CDs-MPs) with clean surfaces and concentrated particle sizes were obtained by soap-free microemulsion polymerization. The concentration of CDs-MPs exhibits a significant linear relationship with long-wavelength fluorescence intensity (λEx/λEm: 415/676 nm). Soybean sprouts (SBS), as an important type of food, are susceptible to contamination of MPs due to their soft epidermis and rapidly growing biomass. The results showed that CDs-MPs could be embedded into the cortex of SBS and enter the plant with cell division and elongation, leading to an increase in pore size on the cell wall surface. After entering the root system, CDs-MPs will pass through the Casparian strip and migrate in the vessels. Then, CDs-MPs enter the leaves through vascular bundles, and the distribution and size of epicuticular wax on leaves have changed. Furthermore, SBS showed resistant growth and increased levels of oxidative response when exposed to MPs/CDs-MPs. It is the first study to demonstrate the application of leaf-derived CDs in the prevention of MPs pollution by revealing the migration behavior of submicrometre plastics in SBS.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: MPs (<5 mm) usually originate from industrial production or degradation of plastic products. The earliest discovery of MPs was in the field of marine ecology, and they have since been detected in rivers, lakes, soil, groundwater, and air. In the environment, they are difficult to completely decompose and will further break down [1] as submicrometre or nano plastics with a larger specific surface area. These particles are easy to enter the biological tissue and accumulate in animals or plants [2]. Nowadays, MPs are already present in human blood [3], placenta [4], and heart [5]. It may come from food, drinking water, or plastic products used in daily life. Micro/nano plastics in these tissues not only produce mechanical damage but even penetrate the blood-brain barrier causing neurotoxicity. Therefore, it is urgent to effectively avoid micro/nano plastics pollution.

According to the current reports, large amounts of MPs are present in a variety of environmental media (atmosphere, water, and soil). Jahandari et al. [6] summarized the current situation of MPs pollution in indoor/outdoor air globally, with concentrations reaching up to 1584/393 items/m3. Concentrations of MPs in rivers are even higher, with concentrations of MPs (PE, PP, PS, PVC, PA, PET, etc.) ranging from 0 to 11,532 items/m3 in major rivers from several countries [7]. These MPs are enriched and transported with atmospheric activity or water flow, and soils are highly likely to be the sources and sinks for MPs. Yan et al. [8] reviewed the contamination of MPs (PE, PP, PS, PVC, PES, Nylon, etc.) in farmlands from various countries, with concentrations ranging from 0.34 to 6.9 × 105 items/kg. With the growth of crops, it has the potential that MPs will enter the human body through the consumption of agricultural products. Conti et al. [9] studied the pollution level of MPs in fruits and vegetables from the markets of Catania (Italy), where the concentration of MPs (<10 µm) ranged from 52,050–233,000 items/g. Therefore, there is an urgent necessity to prevent and control MPs contamination of edible agricultural products.

In general, the cell walls of plants can resist MPs with larger particle sizes, but the risk of MPs pollution significantly increases with decreasing particle size. Submicrometer/nano plastics can easily enter the plant through the holes [10] or cracks [11] on the surface, causing morphology changes in epidermal cells. MPs in plants are able to migrate under transpiration pull. In addition, the functional groups [12] and the charged properties [13] of MPs resulted in differences between their migration and accumulation in plants. MPs stress can affect plant metabolic processes and generate stress responses, leading to an increase in root secretion [14] and biomass [15]. Most of the current research focuses on crops (such as corn, lettuce, wheat, rice, etc.), with less attention paid to SBS, a whole edible plant food obtained through artificial soaking. It is easily susceptible to MPs contamination from the soaking process, which is even more harmful to humans. Understanding the entry and distribution behavior of MPs in the edible plants is crucial for the prevention and control of MPs pollution.

However, the current research methods have significant limitations for submicrometer or nano plastics [16,17], which restrict the development of related research. For example, spectroscopic analysis such as Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy and Raman spectroscopy is hardly applicable to identify plastic particles at the submicrometer and nanometer scales [18,19]. Pyrolysis gas chromatography-mass spectroscopy (Py-GC/MS) is also difficult to effectively analyze cross-linked polymers and copolymers [20,21]. Considering the time-consuming, laborious, and expensive detection by traditional methods [22]. Fluorescent labeling, as an easy-to-operate and low-cost method, has great advantages for studying the environmental behavior of submicron and nano plastic particles.

The application of fluorescence analysis is mainly influenced by the properties of the materials. In the early period, labeling MPs with Nile red dye was the most popular method [23], but it was susceptible to interference. Subsequently, various fluorescent labeling methods were developed. Rare earth luminescent materials have strong emission capabilities based on their multiple energy levels. Plastic microspheres labeled with rare earth materials by solvation-diffusion can be used to trace MPs in wheat and lettuce (λEx/λEm: 360/615 nm) [24]. Then, functional carbon nitride with rigid planar and conjugated structures has aggregation-induced emission (AIE) properties [25] that can be used to quantify the release of MPs in disposable plastic tableware (λEx/λEm: 365/490 nm) [26]. In addition, small molecule fluorescent dyes are frequently used as tracer materials to study MPs in plants, such as 4-chloro-7-nitro-1,2,3-benzodiazole and Nile blue (λEx/λEm: 488/515 nm, 635/680 nm) [27]. The effectiveness of tracer in MPs can be limited due to smaller Stokes shifts, shorter emission wavelengths, and other factors that would affect the resolution.

The CDs are composed of stacked graphene fragments and spherical core structures with surface functional groups. However, research on the environmental behavior of MPs using long-wavelength CDs is scarce. Shebeeb et al. [28] obtained fluorescent plastic particles using candle soot (λEx/λEm: 325/381, 370/422 nm). Zhang et al. [29] synthesized boron-doped carbon nanoparticles from pyrene nitrate and boric acid, which can be used to identify the types of plastics with different fluorescence when fused with plastics. Liang et al. [30] prepared fluorescently labeled plastic microspheres using nitropyridine to synthesize CDs (λEx/λEm: 478/563 nm) and confirmed their low toxicity by experiments on aquatic phytoplankton and zooplankton. According to the existing literature, labeled spheres are susceptible to interference by autofluorescence especially in plants [31]. Compared with traditional fluorescent materials, long-wavelength carbon dots have the advantages of good photoresponse, biocompatibility, and high spatial resolution. Therefore, it is more helpful to develop long-wavelength CDs as labeling materials to study the environmental behavior of MPs.

In this study, eco-friendly and long-wavelength fluorescent CDs were prepared from a natural carbon source (shrub leaves) by solvothermal method, and fluorescent polystyrene microspheres labeled with CDs were obtained by soap-free microemulsion polymerization. The entry process of submicrometre plastics into the tissue of SBS and the influence mechanism were investigated through hydroponics experiments. This study further reveals the interaction relationship between submicrometre plastics and plant tissues, providing theoretical support for the prevention and control of MPs pollution.

NaHCO3 (Aladdin Co., Ltd),K2S2O8 (Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd), Acrylic acid (Adamas Reagent Co., Ltd), Ethanol absolute-EtOH, Kerosene (Shanghai Macklin Biochemical Co., Ltd), Dialysis bag (Nanjing Caobenyuan Biotechnology Co., Ltd, 1000/3500 Da). Styrene, KBr, Ethyl acetate (Meryer Chemical Technology Co., Ltd). Fresh shrub leaves (Buxus megistophylla H. Lév.) from campus were picked in April.

First, the fresh and intact leaves of the shrub were extracted and stored in the refrigerator at − 18 ℃. Rinse three times with tap and distilled water. After that, they were cut into small pieces and soaked in EtOH for 24 h. The leaf-derived extract (mainly composed of chlorophyll) was passed through a 0.22 µm glass fiber membrane (GF/F). Subsequently, the solution was evaporated by rotary evaporation at 50 ℃. This was immediately followed by freeze-drying at − 40 ℃ to obtain chlorophyll powder. The powder (0.15 g) was dissolved in 30 mL of EtOH and ultrasonicated for 20 min. Then it was placed in a 50 mL high-pressure reactor and reacted for 8 h. At the end of the reaction, the solution was filtered by a 0.22 µm membrane filter, and the CDs were obtained by rotary evaporation and freeze-drying. All CDs were stored in the refrigerator.

The submicrometre plastics (100–1000 nm) were obtained by soap-free microemulsion polymerization ( Fig. 1), using potassium persulfate as the thermal decomposition initiator and acrylic acid as the comonomer. Soap-free emulsion polymerization was able to obtain polymer microspheres with good monodispersity, clean surface, and functional groups. 0.01 g of CDs were taken and dissolved in 2 mL of styrene, ultrasonicated for 20 min, and passed through a 0.22 µm membrane filter. The initiator was dissolved in deionized water and placed in a flask, and NaHCO3 was added to maintain the stability and pH of the reaction. The acrylic acid was added when the reaction temperature was reached (70 ℃), and 1.5 mL of styrene with CDs was added slowly. The reaction was carried out under the protection of N2 for 7 h (Fig. 1 and Table S1). The emulsion was naturally cooled down and stored at room temperature away from light. The 3 mL polymer emulsion was placed in a glass sample bottle and dried in an oven at 60 ℃ for 24 h. The solid content was obtained according to the mass difference (0.0251 ± 0.0028 g/mL).

Transmission electron microscopy (TEM, JEM-F200) and X-ray diffraction (XRD, Panalytical-Aeris) were used to observe the morphology and lattice structure of chlorophyll-CDs and CDs-MPs. The functional groups on the surface were characterized using Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FTIR, PerkinElmer). The surface of the samples was scanned using X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS, Thermo Scientific ESCALAB Xi+). The average hydrodynamic diameter and zeta potential of CDs-MPs were measured using a Malvern zetasizer (Nano ZS90). The synthesized CDs as well as CDs-MPs were diluted to obtain fluorescence spectra using a fluorescence spectrophotometer. The thermal stability of CDs-MPs was analyzed using a Thermogravimetric-differential scanning calorimeter (TG-DSC, Netzsch STA449F5) under argon atmosphere.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The exposure of CDs-MPs in soybean sprouts
ce:section.ce:para: Full-grained soybeans were selected, alcohol sterilized, washed with deionized water, and finally placed in sterile Petri dishes for soaking overnight. The soybean was then placed in a nursery pot for sprouting and evenly sprayed using distilled water to maintain humidity.

The emulsion was dialyzed for 6 h with a dialysis bag before further experiments. When soybean roots grew to 2–3 cm, the exposure experiments (CDs-MPs concentration of 50 mg/L, pH=7.02 ± 0.62) were conducted in the dark to obtain etiolated seedlings. All solutions were changed daily, and 5 parallel samples were prepared. All blank groups were treated with distilled water.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Observation and measurement of bean sprouts
ce:section.ce:para: Roots, stems, and leaves were taken after 5 days and rinsed with ultrapure water. The samples were sectioned using the freehand sectioning method and the structures of the plant tissues were observed using an optical microscope (Leica-DM6000B). The entire seeding was fixed with FAA fixative (50 %), frozen section, and imaged using a confocal laser scanning microscope (CLSM, Zeiss-LSM800) with excitation and emission wavelengths set at 415 nm and 676 nm, respectively. Scanning electron microscopy (FE-SEM, Hitachi-SU8010) was utilized for observing the section of plant tissues after being frozen (in liquid nitrogen) and freeze-dried. Sample data collection using the five-point sampling method.

Biochemical indices (Catalase, CAT; Peroxidase, POD; Superoxide dismutase, SOD; Malondialdehyde, MDA) in tissues were measured by detection kits (Beijing Solarbio Science & Technology Co., Ltd, China) to determine the effects of oxidative stress induced by CDs-MPs.

The data were statistically analyzed using SPSS software. Differences in data under different treatments were calculated based on one-way ANOVA, LSD post hoc test, and Duncan test. Differences were considered significant when p < 0.05.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The characterization and fluorescence properties of leaf-derived CDs
ce:section.ce:para: The characterization of leaf-derived carbon dots is shown in Fig. 2(A). The CDs obtained after the solvothermal reaction were quasi-spherical particles with an average particle size of about 3.30 nm (Fig. 2a). Its interplanar crystal spacing is 0.21 nm, which corresponds to the (100) diffraction facets of graphitic carbon (Fig. 2b). The results of X-ray diffraction are shown in Fig. 2c. The diffraction peaks at 28.42° and 40.55° correspond to the (002) and (100) crystal planes of the graphite structure. In particular, the broader peak near 20° indicates the presence of amorphous carbon structure [32]. The carbon dots synthesized from leaf extract as a carbon source have a lower and more concentrated particle size, as well as a more pronounced graphitic carbon structure, compared to those synthesized through chemical carbon sources such as o-phenylenediamine [33] and pyrene [34].

The infrared spectral information of chlorophyll and CDs can be obtained from Fig. 2d. The infrared spectra of chlorophyll were the most informative, with absorption peaks at 3500–3200 cm−1 for the overlapping stretching vibrations of O-H and N-H. The -CH2- is observed at 2930 and 2858 cm−1. The two absorptions around 1500 cm−1 mean the -C-N stretching vibrations. The peak near 1067 cm−1 is attributed to -C-O- stretching vibrations.

With chlorophyll carbonization, graphitic carbon is formed. The peak at ∼1150 cm−1 corresponds to the gradual disappearance of the C-O-C of the ester group, indicating that the ester group of chlorophyll decomposes gradually with increasing temperature. The peaks at ∼1653 cm−1 and 1610–1400 cm−1 are attributed to the shift of CO and CN/CC to lower wave numbers, probably due to the increased graphitization and conjugation of the CO/CN bonds with double bonds or benzene rings. The content of N and Mg elements in CDs based on chlorophyll synthesis is relatively low, and the signal is weak (Fig. S1).

In organic solutions, the transmitted light of chlorophyll is green, while the reflected light is red. Chlorophyll is a magnesium porphyrin compound. A large π-bonded conjugated system with rigidity is composed of four pyrrole rings which are linked by four methylene groups. This conjugation effect and rigid planar structure can limit the vibration and rotation of atoms in molecule, hindering the energy dissipation of excited state molecules, and providing the basis for electron radiative transitions [35]. The conjugated double bond and the central magnesium atom on the porphyrin ring are susceptible to electron gain and loss caused by optical excitation. The excitation wavelength of light is near 415 nm or 665 nm with an intense red-light emission near 672 nm (Fig. S2).

Based on the above foundation, the fluorescence spectra of CDs synthesized with different masses of chlorophyll are shown in Fig. 2(B). The emission wavelengths of chlorophyll CDs under different excitation conditions are all around 676 nm, indicating that leaf derived CDs have the same central structure as chlorophyll. Among them, when the weight of added chlorophyll is 0.025 g, the excitation spectrum of CDs is closest to the excitation spectrum of chlorophyll. As the weight of chlorophyll added increased, the peaks at the lower wavelengths in the excitation spectrum gradually disappeared, while the relative peaks at higher wavelengths increased. However, the increase in concentration leads to a decrease in the peak of the emission spectrum, and the optimal emission wavelength increases from 676 nm to 686 nm, indicating a red shift in the emission center.

The fluorescence intensity decreases with increasing concentration, which is naturally associated with aggregation-caused quenching (ACQ). The aggregation of molecules increases the transfer of energy and reduces radiative excursions. Further confirmation shows that the excitation and emission spectra obtained by diluting the CDs synthesized at a high concentration (0.15 g) were the same as those synthesized with 0.05 g of chlorophyll (Fig. S3). Therefore, the concentration of chlorophyll has few effects on the degree of carbonization here.

As the reaction temperature increases, it can be observed that the peaks of the excitation spectra are sharper and increase in intensity, indicating that the degree of carbonization is strengthened. Correspondingly, the fluorescence emission intensity is also enhanced. From the emission spectra, it can be found that the emission center moves to 680 nm when the reaction temperature rises, which helps to produce CDs with better fluorescence capability. However, Qin et al. [36] synthesized fluorescent carbon dots using camphor leaf powder, and their fluorescence emission changed from red to blue with the temperature increased. This is attributed to the formation of new luminescent centers by other substances (e.g., cellulose, lignin) in the leaf powder. Compared with small molecule fluorescent materials and rare earth fluorescent materials commonly used in current research, leaf-derived carbon dots have advantages such as large Stokes shift, long-wavelength emission, high signal-to-noise ratio, sensitive photoresponse, and good biocompatibility [37].

As shown in Fig. 3, polystyrene microspheres containing CDs were prepared by soap-free polymerization. The polymerization is completed in the formation of micelles by stirring. The soap-free emulsion polymerization has significant advantages because the cleaner and more concentrated particle size distribution of microspheres can be obtained without the use of emulsifiers. According to Fig. 3(a–f), the particle size of CDs-MPs is 217.98 ± 14.13 nm (The hydrodynamic diameter is 229.72 ± 4.88 nm), and the zeta potential is − 42.05 ± 0.99 mV (Fig. S4). The results of EDS illustrate the uniform elemental distribution of the microspheres, as shown in Fig. 3(g–l). The shell and core structure formed by polymerization could be clearly seen under the TEM. According to the micelle nucleation theory of oligomers, the particle growth in the post nucleation stage occurs in a shell region, with a shell thickness (10–40 nm) approximately equal to the root mean square of the free radical chain length [38]. The most of -SO3H\-COOH groups at the end of the polymer chain are distributed on the particle surface. The TG-DSC was utilized to determine the thermal stability and composition of the materials. As shown in Fig. 3(m–o), the test was conducted from room temperature to 800 ℃ with a total weight loss of 91.68 % over four stages. The decomposition temperature of the CDs-MPs was 350–400 ℃, and the weight loss was 80.44 % at this stage.

The FTIR spectra of CDs-MPs are shown in Fig. 3(p). The -CH- stretching vibration on the benzene ring exhibits at 3000–3100 cm−1. The saturated -CH and -CH2- stretching vibrations were observed at 2800–3000 cm−1. The vibrations at 1493 and 1600 cm−1 are skeletal vibrations of the benzene ring, and the out-of-plane vibrations of H on the monosubstituted benzene ring were observed at 700 and 760 cm−1. The peaks at 1712 cm−1 are the characteristic O-CO on the acrylic acid, which is the result of the copolymerization of styrene with acrylic acid. The absorption peak around 1200 cm−1 is an asymmetric stretching vibration absorption of -SO in sulfonyl groups.

Except for the characteristic peaks of polystyrene, there are more significant differences in the infrared spectra of polystyrene microspheres with or without encapsulated CDs at 1635 and 1560 cm−1 of CO and C-N. The more obvious characteristic peaks on the CDs (1375 and 1067 cm−1) enhanced with the increase of carbon dots addition. Besides, the absorption peaks at 1312 and 985 cm−1 were also enhanced, indicating better binding of CDs and polystyrene.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Linear fitting between emulsion concentration and fluorescence intensity
ce:section.ce:para: The fluorescence spectrum of the polystyrene emulsion is shown in Fig. 4(a–b). Polystyrene is a common plastic material consisting of saturated carbon chains and conjugated benzene rings as side groups. The polystyrene has a strong frequency doubling peak, resulting in a more intense interference when scanning the fluorescence spectrum. Thus, the position of the frequency doubling peak needs to be avoided.

The optimal excitation wavelength was obtained based on the peak position of the excitation spectrum, and then the optimal emission wavelength was obtained based on the optimal excitation conditions. Thus, the optimal excitation of the microsphere is obtained at 415 nm and the optimal emission at 676 nm. Compared with current research on MPs using Nile Red [12] and rare earth materials [24] as labeling materials or commercially available fluorescent plastic microspheres [39], the microspheres prepared in this study have larger Stokes shifts and longer emission wavelengths.

The relationship between emulsion concentration and fluorescence intensity at 415 nm was further examined. Based on the fluorescence emission intensity of solutions with different concentrations of CDs-MPs, Fig. 4c was obtained. The fluorescence intensity decreased when the concentration reached 10,040 mg/L (400 μL/mL), which may be caused by the decrease in light transmittance due to the increase in concentration. There is a strong linear relationship as the concentration increases from 10 to 3012 mg/L (y = 197.0717 +2.6387x, R2 =0.9840, Fig. 4d). Therefore, it could be used to determine the concentration of the emulsion by measuring its fluorescence intensity, which is convenient for experiments related to MPs contamination. Polystyrene has fluorescence excitation and emission phenomena due to the large number of benzene rings, providing the basis for fluorescence resonance (energy resonance). This structure contributes to the enhancement of fluorescence phenomena of the encapsulated CDs.

In addition, the polystyrene structure provides a weakly polar/non-polar environment. And the CDs are more dispersed by combining with the polymer, reducing the ACQ phenomenon and improving fluorescence efficiency [40,41].

ce:section.ce:section-title: The uptake behavior of CDs-MPs by soybean sprouts
ce:section.ce:para: Soybean is a type of cotyledon growing plant with faster hypocotyl growth. Sprouted beans, a popular vegetable product, are obtained by the soaking-sprouting process. In this study, using the same soaking process, soybeans were cultivated away from light. In the darkroom, the proplastid in the cotyledons develops into leucoplast or etioplast, and yellowish etiolated seedlings are obtained. Its microstructure is shown in Fig. S5.

After 5 days of cultivation, the exposure to CDs-MPs (50 mg/L) did not significantly inhibit the growth rate of soybean. As SBS grows fast and the plant volume increases rapidly, it creates the potential for the entry of MPs. Following the adhesion effect, the MPs attach tightly to the surface of the SBS root. With the increase in root diameter, cell elongation, and lateral root growth [11], MPs are able to enter the plant through cracks in the root surface. MPs entering the plant can further exacerbate contamination by occupying space [10], leading to increased cell gaps and tearing of the pectin layer.

The roots, stems, and leaves of the seedlings were cut transversely and placed under CLSM for observation, and the results are shown in Fig. 5. Under CDs-MPs exposed, the distribution of fluorescent microspheres could be clearly observed in the roots. The CDs-MPs are basically distributed at the epidermis, parenchyma cell, and vascular bundles. It means that CDs-MPs could enter the plant through the epidermis of the roots or the interstices of the lateral root. Except for the cortex as well as parenchyma cell tissues, the fluorescence signal was strongest at the Casparian strip, indicating that CDs-MPs are most concentrated here. The Casparian strip, a well-aligned and tightly packed band of cells in the endothelium, can prevent the apoplastic pathway transport between the cortex and the vascular bundle, allowing only water and inorganic salts to enter the vascular bundle via the symplastic pathway. For that reason, a large amount of CDs-MPs can accumulate here. There are several types of ionic groups (amines, carboxyl groups, phenols, or phosphate groups) in the composition of root cell walls that can participate in ion exchange [42]. Except for ions, these groups on the cell wall also affect the migration and distribution of MPs in plants. Sun et al. [13] demonstrated that negatively charged MPs can be taken up by plants and accumulate in the mid-column, which is similar to the results we obtained.

Distinct from the blank controls, CDs-MPs were distributed in the cortex and vascular bundles of the stems. This is related to the growth of SBS and the migration of CDs-MPs in the plant. During the rapid growth of SBS, stem elongation and cell gap enlargement, CDs-MPs migrate and distribute in the plant through the apoplastic pathway and symplastic pathway.

Fluorescence interference was most visible in the leaves, but the fluorescence intensity of chlorophyll precursor substances in the plastids was significantly weaker, whereas the fluorescence of CDs-MPs had longer wavelengths of emission and higher fluorescence efficiencies. Therefore, the fluorescence signals in the leaves of seedlings under CDs-MPs exposure were stronger and redder in color. There were obvious fluorescence signals at the main leaf veins in the middle of the leaves, which means that MPs can migrate from roots and stems to leaves through vascular bundles. The particle size, charge, and surface functional groups of MPs all influence their distribution processes in plants. Zhu et al. [12] researched the entry behavior of different particle sizes and carboxyl/amino functional groups in wheat, pointing out that the internalization process of MPs is influenced by both particle size and surface functional groups.

The fluorescent microspheres labeled with long-wavelength emission CDs can avoid the fluorescence interference of lignin [43]. However, it can be observed that seedlings without CDs-MPs have significant fluorescence interference in stems and leaves. The fluorescence disturbance in the stem mainly comes from meristematic cells, which can develop into assimilating tissues through photosynthesis. And the fluorescence interference in the leaves comes mostly from chlorophyll synthesis precursors (e.g., pchlide) in the etioplast. Generally, chlorophyll is generated from proplastids in the meristems of young plants, which will differentiate into different plastids in different environments and tissues, mainly including etioplasts, amyloplasts, chromoplastids, and chloroplasts.

The characterization of CDs-MPs using FE-SEM based on CLSM imaging. As shown in Fig. 6, the internalization of MPs in the roots and stems could be observed clearly after CDs-MPs exposure. In the roots, the distribution of MPs was more obvious due to the long exposure time and large exposure area, which is consistent with the results of CLSM. However, it was difficult to observe the same distinct phenomenon in longitudinal section samples of roots, which was limited by sample preparation methods and cell morphology (Fig. S6). The plant cell wall is usually a porous matrix composed of polysaccharide fibers. CDs-MPs in water are first embedded into the cortex, and as seedling cells divide and elongate, larger pores can be generated on the surface of the cell wall (Fig. S7). CDs-MPs can enter the interior of plants and continue to be dispersed within the plant based on this process. Except for internalization, the adhesion of MPs causes physical blockage and affects the transfer of material and capacity between the cell and the environment [44]. Root exudates (small-molecule acids, alcoholates, amino acids, etc.) can also alter the morphology, carbon/oxygen ratio, charge, and other properties of MPs as natural ligands to exhibit more unpaired electrons, resulting in enhanced biotoxicity [45].

There seem to be fewer CDs-MPs in the stems, but the distribution of microspheres could still be observed on the inner wall. Leaves are difficult to visually distinguish due to the presence of microscopic hydrophobic [46] and supporting structures such as epicuticular wax and mastoid. The samples without CDs-MPs treatment had an even distribution of epicuticular wax and mastoid on the surfaces with symmetrical sizes. However, the surface waxes of the samples under CDs-MPs exposure were irregular in size and distribution, which is consistent with the changes in plant structure and physiology induced by MPs stress. Sun et al. [47] pointed out that epidermal internalization and stomata were the main entry routes for MPs on the leaf surface, which can have an impact on the physiological structure of leaves.

The vessel is the key conducting tissue in plants for delivering water and inorganic salts. According to their formation and development, they can be classified as ringed vessels, spiral vessels, scalariform vessels, reticulated vessels, and pitted vessels. In particular, ringed vessels and spiral vessels differentiated earlier, and these vessels have small pore sizes and relatively weak transport capacity and are generally found in the primary xylem. According to Fig. 7(a–l), the red fluorescence is mainly on the vessel and the surrounding cell wall. The signal is stronger on the vessel, which indicates more CDs-MPs aggregation at the vessel. According to Fig. 7(m–o), we observed that circular folds in the vessel can be more advantageous for the accumulation of MPs. The soybean is an annual herb with underdeveloped xylem and secondary structures in its herbaceous stems. The CDs-MPs enter the plant through the root tip epidermis and the lateral root gap and are transported along the vessel in the plant. MPs with -SO3H, -OH, -COOH, and -NH2 functional groups on their surfaces are able to exclude elements with the same surface charge or attract elements with opposite charges, interfering with the uptake and translocation of nutrients by plants [48]. The accumulation of CDs-MPs at the vessel can lead to restricted water and nutrient transfer to the plant, which can affect the growth and development of the plant.

According to current research, plastic containers usually cause MPs contamination during contact with water. For example, the release during the use of plastic food containers is 3–29 items/container [49], and the release of microplastics/nanoplastics during the use of carbonated beverage bottles [50] can be as high as 234–9.21 × 107 items/L. The soybean and SBS are widely used as common ingredients, and the seedlings grow rapidly during the soaking process. Therefore, it is highly likely that MPs from water and plastic containers will be ingested, resulting in MPs contamination from food.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Physiological and biochemical effects of CDs-MPs on soybean sprouts
ce:section.ce:para: MPs stress causes morphological structural and physiological changes in SBS. As shown in Fig. 8(a–d), the stress of CDs-MPs increased the moisture content of bean sprouts by 9.97 %, while the plant height increased by 15.49 %. In addition, seedlings exposed to CDs-MPs showed an increase in lateral root length (12.84 %) and number (28.81 %). And labeling of CDs did not make a significant difference in growth. Under the exposure of MPs, soybean seedlings showed significant growth of stress resistance, as evidenced by an increase in plant height and lateral roots. This is due to the occupancy effect of MPs hindering the material exchange between plants and the environment, and plants maintain normal physiological activities through compensatory growth. And the occupying effect also leads to the increase of cell gap, the tearing of the pectin layer, and the deformation of the root tip, increasing of moisture content. Besides the structural alterations caused by the occupancy effect of MPs, the effect on the metabolic behavior of the plant also leads to changes in the growth behavior. Liu et al. [51] also found that MPs promoted the increase of plant height and biomass in cucumbers. Moreover, MPs also reduced the rate of lignin synthesis affecting the structure and stability of cell walls and membranes. Zhao et al. [52] pointed out that the presence of PS and PP could promote the formation of plant inter-root metabolites while having a growth-promoting effect on plant growth. Therefore, it is difficult to recognize whether the soybean sprouts are contaminated or not from appearance.

The stress of submicrometer plastic microspheres leads to stress response in plants. Plants will enhance tissue resistance by maintaining the integrity of cell membranes, accumulating osmoregulatory substances, reactive oxygen species (ROS) balance, and expressing antistress genes. The stress of negative factors causes the accumulation of ROS in plant tissues, which can result in damage to plant tissues. The ROS in plants mainly comes from the electron transfer process in mitochondria and chloroplasts, and the level is low under ordinary circumstances. However, MPs in plant tissues have a strong adsorption capacity with many surface functional groups, which can affect ion exchange, lead to protein aggregation, and cause abnormal enzyme functions. Abnormalities in plant metabolic processes result in the blockage of electron transfer, which leads to an increase in the concentration of ROS. Excessive ROS can further lead to oxidative damage in plant tissues. In particular, CAT, POD, and SOD are the main enzymes for ROS scavenging in plants, which can reflect the antioxidant capacity of the seedling. According to Fig. 8(e–h), the enzymatic activities of CAT, POD, and SOD were increased by 74.75 %, 97.63 %, and 37.32 %, respectively, after exposure to CDs-MPs. This phenomenon indicates that bean sprouts have increased antioxidant content, which minimizes the damage caused by ROS to the tissues. In contrast, oxygen radicals react with unsaturated fatty acids of lipids to generate lipid peroxidation. The content of MDA reflects the degree of peroxidative damage in the tissues. According to the results, the MDA content decreased by 31.75 %, indicating that the oxidative damage was reduced under stress response. Wang et al. [14] pointed out that MPs disrupt the antioxidant system of plants and alter the expression of genes in the root. In addition, MPs can enter cells via clathrin-dependent endocytosis, leading to protein aggregation and causing lysosomal dysfunction [53]. Li et al. [54] found that MPs can interfere with electron transfer in metabolic processes, leading to electron accumulation and oxidative stress damage, which in turn can affect vital movements such as energy production, carbon fixation, lipid metabolism, and nucleic acid metabolism. Particle size, concentration, and electrically charged properties are the main factors causing changes in the physiological and biochemical behavior of plants. A comprehensive study of the physiological and biochemical effects of submicron plastics on SBS is also needed.

The contamination of edible plants with MPs is a critical issue for humans. The entry and contamination process of MPs in edible SBS was comprehensively revealed using leaf-derived long-wavelength carbon dots. The particle size of labeled submicrometre plastics was 217.98 ± 14.13 nm, and a significant linear relationship was found between their concentration and long-wavelength fluorescence intensity (10–3012 mg/L, R2 =0.9840; λEx/λEm:415/676 nm). The rapid growth rate of SBS, cell division, and elongation resulted in increased intercellular space, which increased the possibility of MPs contamination. The CDs-MPs can enter the root system through the epidermis and accumulate in the Casparin strip. The CDs-MPs were able to migrate upward in vessels and enter the leaves through vascular bundles. Then, the distribution and size of the epidermal waxes on the leaves were changed. Microplastic exposure increased the level of oxidative response in bean sprout tissues, but growth inhibition was difficult to observe. These results could provide a scientific basis for further research on the submicrometre/nano plastic pollution process and risk assessment in plants. This work fills a gap in research on MPs with long-wavelength carbon dots applications.

Minimal study exists on revealing the environmental behavior of microplastics using long-wavelength CDs. The long wavelength fluorescence emission of leaf-derived CDs is stable and has a large Stokes shift. This study investigated the entry behavior of long-wavelength CDs labeled microplastics in soybean sprouts. The soybean and bean sprouts are widely used as common food ingredients, and the seedlings grow rapidly during the soaking process. Therefore, it is highly likely that microplastics from water or plastic containers will be ingested, resulting in microplastic contamination from food sources. How to effectively prevent food-source microplastic contamination remains a major challenge.

Chao Liu: Investigation, Data curation, Methodology, Writing-Original draft, Formal analysis. Yuan Jiao: Conceptualization, Methodology, Supervision, Writing-Original draft, Formal analysis. Junmei Guo: Methodology, Validation. Bo Li: Methodology, Validation. Changxin Gu: Data analysis. Tianwei Qian: Conceptualization, Funding acquisition, Supervision, Writing - review & editing. Xiaona Liu: Conceptualization, Supervision, Writing - review & editing.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was financially supported by the Fundamental Research Program of Shanxi Province (No. 202303021221041, No. 20210302124056), Science and Technology Innovation Project of Colleges and Universities in Shanxi Province (2021L029).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data associated with this article can be found in the online version at doi:10.1016/j.jhazmat.2024.134272.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:

.
